stem: Use the Euclidean algorithm to compute \( \gcd([[A]], [[B]]) \). What is the greatest common divisor of \( [[A]] \) and \( [[B]] \)?
solution: The Euclidean algorithm repeatedly replaces the larger number by its remainder upon division by the smaller; the last nonzero remainder is \( \gcd([[A]], [[B]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the product of the cofactors, not the common divisor itself.
This is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[A]],[[B]]) \); the greatest common divisor is much smaller.
This is one more than the correct gcd.
This is one less than the correct gcd.
This is two more than the correct gcd.
This is two less than the correct gcd.
This is three more than the correct gcd.

stem: Applying the Euclidean algorithm pairwise, find \( \gcd([[A]], [[B]], [[C]]) \), the greatest common divisor of all three numbers \( [[A]] \), \( [[B]] \), and \( [[C]] \).
solution: Apply the Euclidean algorithm to two of the numbers first, then to that result and the third: \( \gcd([[A]],[[B]],[[C]]) = \gcd(\gcd([[A]],[[B]]), [[C]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This stops after only the first pairwise gcd, ignoring the third number.
This uses only the second and third numbers, ignoring the first.
This multiplies the cofactors instead of taking a gcd.
This is one more than the correct three-way gcd.
This is one less than the correct three-way gcd.
This is two more than the correct three-way gcd.
This is three more than the correct three-way gcd.

stem: Running the extended Euclidean algorithm on \( [[A]] \) and \( [[B]] \) produces integers \( x \) and \( y \) with \( [[A]]x + [[B]]y = \gcd([[A]], [[B]]) \). What is the smallest positive value of \( x \) that works?
solution: Dividing through by \( d=[[d]] \), the identity reduces to \( [[m]]x \equiv 1 \pmod{[[n]]} \), i.e. \( x \) is the inverse of \( [[m]] \) modulo the prime \( [[n]] \). Since \( [[n]] \) is prime, Fermat's Little Theorem gives \( x \equiv [[m]]^{[[n]]-2} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[n]]} \), the smallest positive such \( x \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( m \) itself, not the Bezout coefficient \( x \).
This is \( n-m \), an unrelated combination.
This is \( m^{2} \bmod n \), not the inverse \( m^{-1} \bmod n \).
This is one more than the correct coefficient.
This is one less than the correct coefficient.
This is two more than the correct coefficient.
This reflects the correct coefficient as \( n - x \), the wrong sign convention.

stem: For a positive integer \( n \), let \( d = \gcd(n + [[b]], \, [[c]]n + [[e]]) \). Running the Euclidean algorithm algebraically (eliminating \( n \) between the two expressions) shows \( d \) is bounded by a fixed constant. What is the largest possible value of \( d \) as \( n \) ranges over the positive integers?
solution: Since \( d \) divides both \( n+[[b]] \) and \( [[c]]n+[[e]] \), it divides \( [[c]](n+[[b]]) - ([[c]]n+[[e]]) = [[c]]\cdot[[b]] - [[e]] \), a fixed constant independent of \( n \). So \( d \le |[[c]]\cdot[[b]] - [[e]]| = [[answer]] \), and choosing \( n \) so that \( n+[[b]] \) is a multiple of \( [[answer]] \) achieves this bound exactly. The largest possible value is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the constants directly, forgetting to scale \( [[b]] \) by the coefficient \( [[c]] \) first.
This multiplies the wrong pair of terms before subtracting.
This adds the constants instead of eliminating \( n \) by subtraction.
This factors out \( [[c]] \) from the wrong combination of terms.
This is one more than the correct bound.
This is one less than the correct bound.
This overshoots the correct bound by the coefficient \( [[c]] \).

stem: What is \( \gcd([[A]], [[B]]) \), i.e. \( \gcd(2^{[[m]]} - 1,\, 2^{[[n]]} - 1) \)? (Running the Euclidean algorithm on these two numbers mirrors running it on the exponents \( [[m]] \) and \( [[n]] \) themselves.)
solution: The Euclidean algorithm on \( 2^{[[m]]}-1 \) and \( 2^{[[n]]}-1 \) proceeds in lockstep with the Euclidean algorithm on the exponents \( [[m]] \) and \( [[n]] \), which gives the identity \( \gcd(2^{a}-1, 2^{b}-1) = 2^{\gcd(a,b)}-1 \). Here \( \gcd([[m]],[[n]]) \) computes to give \( \gcd([[A]], [[B]]) = 2^{\gcd([[m]],[[n]])} - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the exponents instead of taking their gcd.
This subtracts the exponents instead of taking their gcd.
This is \( 2^{[[m]]}-1 \) itself, not the gcd of the two numbers.
This is \( 2^{[[n]]}-1 \) itself, not the gcd of the two numbers.
This is one more than the correct gcd.
This is one less than the correct gcd.
This is two more than the correct gcd.
